title: Pancreatic Cancer
oneLiner: Aggressive exocrine pancreatic malignancy; presents with painless jaundice, weight loss, epigastric pain; 5-year survival <10%.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Ascending cholangitis** (obstructed bile duct + infection): single finding = fever + rigors + jaundice (Charcot’s triad) – do not wait for CT; start antibiotics and arrange urgent ERCP.
- **Acute pancreatitis** (tumour obstruction of pancreatic duct): single finding = epigastric pain radiating to back + vomiting + lipase >3x ULN – supportive care first, CT later.
- **Splenic vein thrombosis with variceal bleeding** (tumour compression): single finding = haematemesis/melaena + splenomegaly on exam – call GI bleed protocol and senior immediately.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Painless jaundice (but often mild epigastric/back pain)
  - Pruritus
  - Dark urine, pale stools
  - Unintentional weight loss (>5% in 6 months)
  - Anorexia, early satiety
  - New-onset diabetes (in elderly non-obese)
  - Vague epigastric discomfort radiating to back
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Jaundice (scleral, skin)
  - Palpable, non-tender gallbladder (Courvoisier’s sign)
  - Epigastric mass (later stages)
  - Ascites (peritoneal disease)
  - Supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow’s node, left side)
  - Hepatomegaly (metastases)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Cholangiocarcinoma** – similar jaundice, but often intrahepatic; imaging shows bile duct stricture; CA19-9 may be elevated in both.
- **Chronic pancreatitis** – pain more constant, imaging shows calcification, duct dilatation; no significant weight loss.
- **Gallstone disease** – RUQ pain, Murphy’s sign positive; ultrasound shows stones; jaundice intermittent.
- **Pancreatic pseudocyst** – history of acute pancreatitis; CT shows fluid collection; no solid mass.
- **Lymphoma** – painless jaundice, but CT shows para-aortic lymphadenopathy; LDH elevated.
- **Splitter:** Courvoisier’s law – palpable gallbladder in jaundice suggests malignancy (tumour), not stones; non-palpable gallbladder favours gallstones.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dip: bilirubin positive, urobilinogen negative (obstructive jaundice)
  - Bedside ultrasound (if available): dilated bile ducts, gallstones, mass? Not definitive
- **Bloods:**
  - LFTs: raised bilirubin, ALP, GGT (obstructive pattern); transaminases may rise later
  - CA19-9 (not diagnostic, but elevated >100 U/mL suggests malignancy; also useful for monitoring)
  - FBC, U+E, LFT, INR, albumin
  - Amylase/lipase (only if pancreatitis suspected)
  - Coagulation screen (INR for biliary obstruction/history of stent)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: CT pancreas (triple phase) – assesses tumour size, vascular involvement, metastasis
  - Second-line: MRCP if CT equivocal for bile duct stricture
  - If metastatic: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for staging
- **Special tests:**
  - EUS + biopsy (for histological confirmation when CT non-diagnostic or for tissue diagnosis before chemotherapy)
  - ERCP (therapeutic – stent insertion; can take brushings for cytology)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Histology from biopsy/brushings is gold standard; imaging alone not sufficient for neoadjuvant/chemotherapy decisions.
- **Never-miss test: CT abdomen with pancreas protocol – without it you cannot stage or plan treatment; omission leads to delay and missed resectability window.**
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If cholangitis: IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q8h) + urgent ERCP – call GI team
  - If acute pancreatitis: NBM, IV fluids (0.9% NaCl 1L stat then 250 mL/h), analgesia (paracetamol ± morphine), monitor observations
  - If variceal bleed: call GI bleed team; terlipressin + antibiotics + urgent endoscopy
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Confirm staging: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, consider PET-CT if borderline resectable
  - Discuss at MDT (hepatobiliary surgeon, oncologist, radiologist, pathologist)
  - Biliary drainage: ERCP with stent (metal for resectable, plastic for palliation if short-term)
  - Pain management: stepwise WHO ladder – paracetamol → NSAIDs (avoid if renal impairment/bleeding risk) → opioids (morphine or hydromorphone)
  - Nutrition: oral if tolerated; consider nasojejunal feeding if duodenal obstruction; consider pancreatic enzyme replacement (Creon 25,000-50,000 units with meals)
  - Refer to clinical nurse specialist/dietitian/palliative care if advanced
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Resectable (20%): Whipple procedure (pancreaticoduodenectomy) – refer to hepatobiliary surgeon
  - Locally advanced/borderline resectable: neoadjuvant chemotherapy (FOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel) – oncology referral
  - Metastatic: palliative chemotherapy (gemcitabine-based or FOLFIRINOX); symptom control
- **Default Orders (if admitted for jaundice/obstruction):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% NaCl at maintenance (unless cholangitis/sepsis – then aggressive resuscitation)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (if no thrombocytopenia or active bleeding)
  - Diet: NBM if pancreatitis or impending ERCP; otherwise soft/liquid diet (avoid large meals)
  - Observations: 4-hourly (NEWS2) – if septic or post-ERCP, consider 1-hourly for 6h
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Antibiotics for cholangitis: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q8h (or [check local policy] for alternative)
  - Analgesia: morphine 5-10mg SC/IV q4h PRN (titrate) or hydromorphone 1-2mg SC/IV q4h
  - Antiemetic: ondansetron 4-8mg IV/PO q8h PRN
  - Pancreatic enzymes: Creon (pancreatin) 25,000-50,000 units with each meal (dose based on fat intake)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (or 20mg if eGFR <30 mL/min)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Antibiotics: co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV q8h (if no penicillin allergy)
  - Pain: fentanyl patch for chronic pain
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if renal impairment, GI bleeding risk, or on anticoagulation
  - Opioids: avoid if GCS <15, respiratory depression
  - Chemotherapy (not prescribed by FY1) – but note interaction with warfarin (INR monitoring)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Morphine: reduce dose in renal impairment (accumulation of metabolites)
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min (e.g., 20mg daily)
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: adjust in severe renal impairment (eGFR <20 – give 4.5g q8h? actually q12h – [check local policy])
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Opioid overdose** – especially in elderly or renal impairment: start low (e.g., morphine 2.5-5mg SC), check baseline GCS and respiratory rate; have naloxone available.
  - **Miscalculating chemotherapy doses** – FY1 should never prescribe chemo without double-checking protocol and consultant sign-off; if asked to write up, ask for verification.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Ascending cholangitis (obstructed stent)
  - Acute pancreatitis
  - Duodenal/gastric outlet obstruction (tumour invasion)
  - Variceal bleeding (splenic vein thrombosis)
  - DVT/PE (hypercoagulable state)
  - Chemotherapy-induced neutropenic sepsis
- **Chronic:**
  - Cachexia (malnutrition, fat malabsorption)
  - Depression
  - Ascites (peritoneal disease)
  - Liver failure (biliary obstruction or metastases)
  - Biliary stent occlusion (needs replacement)
sections.Prognosis:
- Overall 5-year survival <10%; median survival for metastatic disease ~6-12 months.
- Resectable: 5-year survival ~20% after Whipple + adjuvant chemotherapy; most relapse within 2 years.
- Locally advanced: median survival 12-18 months with chemoradiotherapy.
- Higher mortality if CA19-9 >1000 U/mL, low albumin, ECOG performance status ≥2.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Check CA19-9 and CEA at baseline** – although not diagnostic, they help monitor response to chemotherapy and detect recurrence.
- **Consider pancreatic cancer in any elderly non-obese patient with new-onset diabetes** – especially if unexplained weight loss, refer for CT pancreas.
- **If CT pancreas not available, order MRCP** – it better delineates bile duct strictures and can detect small tumours.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not delay ERCP in jaundiced patient with cholangitis for CT/MRI** – sepsis kills faster than accurate imaging; treat infection first, drain bile.
- **Do not assume benign jaundice without imaging** – especially in patients >60 years; a negative ultrasound does not exclude pancreatic cancer (CT/MRCP needed).
- **Do not start anticoagulation for DVT/PE without checking platelets** – chemotherapy and metastases can cause thrombocytopenia; risk of bleeding.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Fever, rigors, or worsening jaundice (cholangitis or stent occlusion)
  - Haematemesis or melaena (variceal bleed, tumour erosion)
  - Severe epigastric pain, vomiting (duodenal obstruction or pancreatitis)
  - Shortness of breath, calf pain/swelling (DVT/PE)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Hepatobiliary/MDT clinic within 2 weeks
  - Oncology appointment if indicated
  - Dietitian review if weight loss continues
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (reg/shift lead) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if rising)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L
  - GCS <15 or new confusion
  - Suspected cholangitis (fever + jaundice + hypotension)
  - Acute pancreatitis with organ failure (e.g., respiratory failure, renal failure)
  - Haematemesis/melaena (call GI bleed team and on-call hepatobiliary surgeon)
- **Call ICU team if:**
  - Requires vasopressors (despite fluid resuscitation) or non-invasive ventilation
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Persistent hypotension (MAP <65 mmHg)

rawMarkdown:
## Pancreatic Cancer

**One-liner:** Aggressive exocrine pancreatic malignancy; presents with painless jaundice, weight loss, epigastric pain; 5-year survival <10%.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Ascending cholangitis** (obstructed bile duct + infection): single finding = fever + rigors + jaundice (Charcot’s triad) – do not wait for CT; start antibiotics and arrange urgent ERCP.
- **Acute pancreatitis** (tumour obstruction of pancreatic duct): single finding = epigastric pain radiating to back + vomiting + lipase >3x ULN – supportive care first, CT later.
- **Splenic vein thrombosis with variceal bleeding** (tumour compression): single finding = haematemesis/melaena + splenomegaly on exam – call GI bleed protocol and senior immediately.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Painless jaundice (but often mild epigastric/back pain)
  - Pruritus
  - Dark urine, pale stools
  - Unintentional weight loss (>5% in 6 months)
  - Anorexia, early satiety
  - New-onset diabetes (in elderly non-obese)
  - Vague epigastric discomfort radiating to back
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Jaundice (scleral, skin)
  - Palpable, non-tender gallbladder (Courvoisier’s sign)
  - Epigastric mass (later stages)
  - Ascites (peritoneal disease)
  - Supraclavicular lymph node (Virchow’s node, left side)
  - Hepatomegaly (metastases)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Cholangiocarcinoma** – similar jaundice, but often intrahepatic; imaging shows bile duct stricture; CA19-9 may be elevated in both.
- **Chronic pancreatitis** – pain more constant, imaging shows calcification, duct dilatation; no significant weight loss.
- **Gallstone disease** – RUQ pain, Murphy’s sign positive; ultrasound shows stones; jaundice intermittent.
- **Pancreatic pseudocyst** – history of acute pancreatitis; CT shows fluid collection; no solid mass.
- **Lymphoma** – painless jaundice, but CT shows para-aortic lymphadenopathy; LDH elevated.
- **Splitter:** Courvoisier’s law – palpable gallbladder in jaundice suggests malignancy (tumour), not stones; non-palpable gallbladder favours gallstones.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dip: bilirubin positive, urobilinogen negative (obstructive jaundice)
  - Bedside ultrasound (if available): dilated bile ducts, gallstones, mass? Not definitive
- **Bloods:**
  - LFTs: raised bilirubin, ALP, GGT (obstructive pattern); transaminases may rise later
  - CA19-9 (not diagnostic, but elevated >100 U/mL suggests malignancy; also useful for monitoring)
  - FBC, U+E, LFT, INR, albumin
  - Amylase/lipase (only if pancreatitis suspected)
  - Coagulation screen (INR for biliary obstruction/history of stent)
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: CT pancreas (triple phase) – assesses tumour size, vascular involvement, metastasis
  - Second-line: MRCP if CT equivocal for bile duct stricture
  - If metastatic: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for staging
- **Special tests:**
  - EUS + biopsy (for histological confirmation when CT non-diagnostic or for tissue diagnosis before chemotherapy)
  - ERCP (therapeutic – stent insertion; can take brushings for cytology)
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Histology from biopsy/brushings is gold standard; imaging alone not sufficient for neoadjuvant/chemotherapy decisions.
- **Never-miss test: CT abdomen with pancreas protocol – without it you cannot stage or plan treatment; omission leads to delay and missed resectability window.**

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If cholangitis: IV antibiotics (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q8h) + urgent ERCP – call GI team
  - If acute pancreatitis: NBM, IV fluids (0.9% NaCl 1L stat then 250 mL/h), analgesia (paracetamol ± morphine), monitor observations
  - If variceal bleed: call GI bleed team; terlipressin + antibiotics + urgent endoscopy
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Confirm staging: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, consider PET-CT if borderline resectable
  - Discuss at MDT (hepatobiliary surgeon, oncologist, radiologist, pathologist)
  - Biliary drainage: ERCP with stent (metal for resectable, plastic for palliation if short-term)
  - Pain management: stepwise WHO ladder – paracetamol → NSAIDs (avoid if renal impairment/bleeding risk) → opioids (morphine or hydromorphone)
  - Nutrition: oral if tolerated; consider nasojejunal feeding if duodenal obstruction; consider pancreatic enzyme replacement (Creon 25,000-50,000 units with meals)
  - Refer to clinical nurse specialist/dietitian/palliative care if advanced
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Resectable (20%): Whipple procedure (pancreaticoduodenectomy) – refer to hepatobiliary surgeon
  - Locally advanced/borderline resectable: neoadjuvant chemotherapy (FOLFIRINOX or gemcitabine + nab-paclitaxel) – oncology referral
  - Metastatic: palliative chemotherapy (gemcitabine-based or FOLFIRINOX); symptom control
- **Default Orders (if admitted for jaundice/obstruction):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% NaCl at maintenance (unless cholangitis/sepsis – then aggressive resuscitation)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (if no thrombocytopenia or active bleeding)
  - Diet: NBM if pancreatitis or impending ERCP; otherwise soft/liquid diet (avoid large meals)
  - Observations: 4-hourly (NEWS2) – if septic or post-ERCP, consider 1-hourly for 6h

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Antibiotics for cholangitis: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q8h (or [check local policy] for alternative)
  - Analgesia: morphine 5-10mg SC/IV q4h PRN (titrate) or hydromorphone 1-2mg SC/IV q4h
  - Antiemetic: ondansetron 4-8mg IV/PO q8h PRN
  - Pancreatic enzymes: Creon (pancreatin) 25,000-50,000 units with each meal (dose based on fat intake)
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (or 20mg if eGFR <30 mL/min)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Antibiotics: co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV q8h (if no penicillin allergy)
  - Pain: fentanyl patch for chronic pain
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid if renal impairment, GI bleeding risk, or on anticoagulation
  - Opioids: avoid if GCS <15, respiratory depression
  - Chemotherapy (not prescribed by FY1) – but note interaction with warfarin (INR monitoring)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Morphine: reduce dose in renal impairment (accumulation of metabolites)
  - Enoxaparin: reduce dose if eGFR <30 mL/min (e.g., 20mg daily)
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: adjust in severe renal impairment (eGFR <20 – give 4.5g q8h? actually q12h – [check local policy])
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Opioid overdose** – especially in elderly or renal impairment: start low (e.g., morphine 2.5-5mg SC), check baseline GCS and respiratory rate; have naloxone available.
  - **Miscalculating chemotherapy doses** – FY1 should never prescribe chemo without double-checking protocol and consultant sign-off; if asked to write up, ask for verification.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Ascending cholangitis (obstructed stent)
  - Acute pancreatitis
  - Duodenal/gastric outlet obstruction (tumour invasion)
  - Variceal bleeding (splenic vein thrombosis)
  - DVT/PE (hypercoagulable state)
  - Chemotherapy-induced neutropenic sepsis
- **Chronic:**
  - Cachexia (malnutrition, fat malabsorption)
  - Depression
  - Ascites (peritoneal disease)
  - Liver failure (biliary obstruction or metastases)
  - Biliary stent occlusion (needs replacement)

### Prognosis
- Overall 5-year survival <10%; median survival for metastatic disease ~6-12 months.
- Resectable: 5-year survival ~20% after Whipple + adjuvant chemotherapy; most relapse within 2 years.
- Locally advanced: median survival 12-18 months with chemoradiotherapy.
- Higher mortality if CA19-9 >1000 U/mL, low albumin, ECOG performance status ≥2.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Check CA19-9 and CEA at baseline** – although not diagnostic, they help monitor response to chemotherapy and detect recurrence.
- **Consider pancreatic cancer in any elderly non-obese patient with new-onset diabetes** – especially if unexplained weight loss, refer for CT pancreas.
- **If CT pancreas not available, order MRCP** – it better delineates bile duct strictures and can detect small tumours.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay ERCP in jaundiced patient with cholangitis for CT/MRI** – sepsis kills faster than accurate imaging; treat infection first, drain bile.
- **Do not assume benign jaundice without imaging** – especially in patients >60 years; a negative ultrasound does not exclude pancreatic cancer (CT/MRCP needed).
- **Do not start anticoagulation for DVT/PE without checking platelets** – chemotherapy and metastases can cause thrombocytopenia; risk of bleeding.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Fever, rigors, or worsening jaundice (cholangitis or stent occlusion)
  - Haematemesis or melaena (variceal bleed, tumour erosion)
  - Severe epigastric pain, vomiting (duodenal obstruction or pancreatitis)
  - Shortness of breath, calf pain/swelling (DVT/PE)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Hepatobiliary/MDT clinic within 2 weeks
  - Oncology appointment if indicated
  - Dietitian review if weight loss continues

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (reg/shift lead) if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (especially if rising)
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L
  - GCS <15 or new confusion
  - Suspected cholangitis (fever + jaundice + hypotension)
  - Acute pancreatitis with organ failure (e.g., respiratory failure, renal failure)
  - Haematemesis/melaena (call GI bleed team and on-call hepatobiliary surgeon)
- **Call ICU team if:**
  - Requires vasopressors (despite fluid resuscitation) or non-invasive ventilation
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L
  - Persistent hypotension (MAP <65 mmHg)
